refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, reusable form. The term 'machine-readable model' is domain-appropriate and avoids prescribing how capture occurs (sensor fusion, manual input, CAD import, etc.).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Acceptance: A worksite model artifact exists in a defined schema/format; model contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications for terrain, obstacles, and work zones; model is accessible to planning and execution functions via documented interface.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static and dynamic obstacles within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent authorized work zones and restricted areas.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate captured spatial features into a unified worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.5: Provide read access to the worksite model for planning and execution subsystems.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, positioning) and store in a unified geometric representation (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with feature classification metadata; expose via standardized query interface.

DP2: Decompose worksite into logical zones (safety perimeters, operational areas, hazard regions) and represent each zone as a parameterized geometric primitive (polygon, polyhedron) with associated feature attributes; maintain zone registry and spatial relationships.

DP3: Ingest external site documentation (blueprints, CAD models, safety plans) as reference geometry; register and fuse with real-time sensed data through alignment algorithms; maintain provenance and confidence scores for each feature.

DP4: Implement a graph-based spatial model where nodes represent discrete features (obstacles, landmarks, hazard zones) and edges encode spatial relationships (adjacency, containment, proximity); support incremental updates and query by spatial predicates.

DP5: Capture worksite state as a time-stamped sequence of feature snapshots; each snapshot records geometry, classification, and safety attributes; enable temporal queries and change detection for dynamic environments.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP3 hybrid: Decompose worksite into logical zones (safety perimeters, operational areas, hazard regions) represented as parameterized geometric primitives with feature attributes; fuse reference documentation (blueprints, CAD, safety plans) with real-time sensed data through registration and alignment; maintain a unified zone registry with spatial relationships, provenance, and confidence metadata. Expose via standardized spatial query interface (e.g., containment, proximity, zone membership).

potential_sources: Tight coupling between zone decomposition logic and sensor data ingestion pipeline—risk: changes to zone taxonomy force sensor processing rewrites.

Dependency on external reference geometry (CAD, blueprints) creates implicit coupling to document format and update frequency; misalignment between reference and real-time data can degrade model quality.
Confidence/provenance metadata must be threaded through all fusion steps; inconsistent handling across modules risks silent data quality degradation.
Zone registry and spatial relationship graph may become bottleneck if query patterns are not anticipated; poor indexing can couple downstream planning functions to model performance.
coupling_check.notes: DP2+DP3 is moderately decoupled if zone decomposition is driven by safety/operational policy (external input) rather than sensor capabilities. Fusion algorithm should be isolated as a separate concern. Recommend explicit interface contracts for zone updates and query semantics to minimize downstream coupling.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable and available at model initialization; RISK: If policies change frequently or are ambiguous, zone decomposition becomes brittle.

ASSUMPTION: Reference documentation (blueprints, CAD) is available and reasonably accurate; RISK: Outdated or missing reference data forces reliance on real-time sensing alone, reducing model completeness.

ASSUMPTION: Real-time sensing provides sufficient coverage and accuracy to detect and classify agreed-upon site features; RISK: Sensor blind spots, occlusions, or classification errors create gaps in the model; acceptance criteria may not be met.

ASSUMPTION: Spatial registration between reference and sensed data is achievable with acceptable error bounds; RISK: Large registration errors or drift over time degrade model fidelity and planning reliability.

ASSUMPTION: Zone taxonomy and feature classification scheme are well-defined and stable; RISK: Ambiguous or evolving definitions lead to inconsistent model representation and downstream planning failures.
ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model latency and update frequency; RISK: If real-time responsiveness is required, fusion and query latency may become unacceptable.
ASSUMPTION: Confidence/provenance metadata is actionable by downstream consumers; RISK: If planning functions ignore or misinterpret confidence scores, unsafe decisions may result.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required spatial feature types and classification taxonomy (e.g., obstacles, hazard zones, access points, landmarks); (2) Acceptable model latency and update frequency; (3) Minimum confidence/coverage thresholds for model acceptance; (4) Handling of dynamic features (moving equipment, temporary barriers) vs. static features. Decouple zone decomposition policy from sensor processing by introducing a policy configuration layer. Isolate fusion algorithm as a testable, replaceable module with explicit input/output contracts. Define spatial query interface (e.g., containment, proximity, zone membership) as a contract between model and planning functions to enable independent evolution. Introduce explicit model validation step that checks acceptance criteria (feature coverage, confidence, latency) before exposing model to planning/execution.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial awareness and zone management system responsible for decomposing worksite geometry into logical safety zones, fusing reference documentation with real-time sensed data, maintaining a unified zone registry with spatial relationships and metadata, and exposing standardized spatial query interfaces for zone membership, containment, and proximity assessment.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time sensed data (position, orientation, environmental observations) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to fuse with reference documentation for zone state updates and confidence metadata.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits zone-based safety alerts (zone boundary violations, hazard region entry, proximity warnings) derived from spatial queries against the unified zone registry.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task context and operational area definitions; responds with zone-constrained task feasibility and spatial constraints for task execution planning.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives reference documentation (blueprints, CAD models, safety plans) for registration and alignment with sensed data.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of spatial zone decomposition, hazard region definition, and safety-critical spatial queries. Zone registry, metadata management, and containment/proximity logic are core safety functions. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert emission cross boundaries; reference data fusion is internal.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level orchestrator; embedding zone decomposition and spatial registry here violates separation of concerns. Zone logic would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services for queries and updates. Overly broad and creates circular dependencies.

Very poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/teleoperated asset, not a spatial reasoning system. Zone decomposition and registry belong at system level, not vehicle level. Heavy diffusion: zone queries would need to propagate back to central safety logic; multiple vehicles would duplicate zone state.

Poor fit. Teleoperation manages operator interface and remote control; spatial zone management is orthogonal. Zone logic would diffuse to Safety Services and Vehicle for enforcement. Operator awareness of zones is a consumer, not owner, of zone data.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the single authoritative owner of worksite spatial decomposition, zone registry, and spatial query logic. It ingests real-time telemetry and reference documentation, maintains the unified zone model with provenance and confidence metadata, and exposes standardized spatial interfaces (containment, proximity, membership queries) to Tasking, Teleoperation, and Vehicle subsystems for safety-critical decision-making. This centralizes spatial reasoning and eliminates duplication and inconsistency across distributed agents.
containment_risks: Latency: Real-time fusion of sensed data with reference documentation and spatial query response must meet safety-critical timing constraints; if Safety Services becomes a bottleneck, zone updates may lag vehicle motion.

Scalability: As worksite complexity grows (many zones, high-frequency telemetry), the unified registry and query engine may become computationally intensive; distributed zone caching or hierarchical spatial indexing may be needed.

Reference data synchronization: Blueprints and CAD models may be updated offline; registration and alignment logic must handle stale or conflicting reference data without corrupting the zone registry.
Confidence metadata decay: Confidence in zone state degrades over time without fresh sensed data; Safety Services must define and enforce confidence thresholds for safety-critical queries.
Interface standardization: Spatial query interface (containment, proximity, membership) must be rigorously specified to prevent ad-hoc extensions that diffuse zone logic into consumers.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) incorporation of agreed site features and safety plan/operational policies. Safety Services IM boundary with 0.92 containment score provides clear allocation. Spatial query interface (containment, proximity, membership) is testable and measurable. Real-time fusion and registry state can be validated against reference documentation and sensed data.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. Safety Services is unambiguously designated as the authoritative owner of spatial decomposition, zone registry, and query logic. External interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout, Network Link in) are well-defined and minimize diffusion. The containment hypothesis is explicit and justified. Ownership is singular and non-overlapping with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution orchestrator roles.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP (hybrid DP2+DP3: logical zone decomposition + reference/sensed data fusion + unified registry + standardized spatial query interface) is a sound and complete solution to the FR. It addresses all functional aspects: (1) worksite decomposition into safety perimeters, operational areas, and hazard regions; (2) machine-readable representation via parameterized geometric primitives with attributes; (3) fusion of reference documentation with real-time data; (4) unified registry with spatial relationships, provenance, and confidence metadata; (5) standardized query interface for planning/execution consumers. The DP is logically coherent, non-contradictory, and sufficient to satisfy the FR and acceptance criteria.

diffusion_risks: Latency bottleneck: Real-time fusion of sensed telemetry with reference documentation and spatial query response must meet safety-critical timing constraints (e.g., zone boundary violation detection). If Safety Services becomes a centralized bottleneck, zone state updates may lag vehicle motion, creating safety gaps. Mitigation: define maximum acceptable latency SLA; implement hierarchical spatial indexing and edge caching if needed.

Scalability under high-frequency telemetry: As worksite complexity grows (many zones, dense sensor streams), the unified registry and query engine may become computationally intensive. Distributed zone caching or hierarchical spatial partitioning may be required. Mitigation: design registry with scalable data structures (e.g., R-tree, quadtree); define performance thresholds and scaling strategy upfront.

Reference data synchronization and staleness: Blueprints and CAD models may be updated offline or conflict with sensed reality. Registration and alignment logic must handle stale, incomplete, or conflicting reference data without corrupting the zone registry or triggering false safety alerts. Mitigation: implement versioning, conflict resolution, and confidence decay policies; define thresholds for when reference data is considered unreliable.

Confidence metadata decay and safety-critical thresholds: Confidence in zone state degrades over time without fresh sensed data. Safety Services must define and enforce confidence thresholds for safety-critical queries (e.g., hazard region entry). Unclear thresholds create diffusion risk: consumers may apply ad-hoc confidence logic. Mitigation: standardize confidence decay model and publish thresholds as part of spatial query interface specification.

Interface standardization and ad-hoc extensions: Spatial query interface (containment, proximity, membership) must be rigorously specified to prevent ad-hoc extensions that diffuse zone logic into consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Tasking). Mitigation: freeze interface specification early; enforce strict versioning and change control; audit consumer implementations for non-standard queries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services achieves 0.92 containment score with minimal 0.08 diffusion. The IM boundary is well-scoped: zone decomposition, registry, metadata management, and spatial query logic are internal and cohesive. External interfaces are clean: Telemetry and Network Link are pure inputs; SafetyAlert and Tasking are well-defined outputs/bidirectional contracts. No evidence of zone logic leaking into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE Solution. However, five containment risks must be actively managed to prevent diffusion during implementation: latency, scalability, reference data sync, confidence decay, and interface standardization. These are not IM boundary violations but operational/design challenges that could degrade containment if unaddressed.

recommended_revisions: Define and publish a formal Spatial Query Interface Specification (SQI) that rigidly specifies containment, proximity, and membership query semantics, parameter ranges, response formats, and confidence metadata interpretation. This prevents ad-hoc extensions and diffusion into consumers.

Establish a Confidence Decay Model and Safety Threshold Policy: define how confidence in zone state decays over time without fresh sensed data, and specify minimum confidence thresholds for safety-critical queries (e.g., hazard region entry must have >95% confidence). Publish as part of Safety Services API contract.

Implement a Reference Data Versioning and Conflict Resolution Strategy: define how Safety Services handles offline updates to blueprints/CAD, detects conflicts with sensed reality, and resolves them without corrupting the zone registry. Include rollback and audit trails.

Specify Latency and Scalability SLAs: define maximum acceptable latency for zone state updates and spatial query responses (e.g., <100ms for safety-critical queries). Design hierarchical spatial indexing (R-tree, quadtree) and edge caching strategy to meet SLAs under projected worksite complexity and telemetry frequency.

Formalize the Zone Registry Data Model: specify the schema for zone primitives (geometry, attributes, relationships), spatial relationships (containment, adjacency, overlap), provenance metadata (source, timestamp, confidence), and update semantics (atomic, transactional, eventual consistency). Publish as part of Safety Services design documentation.

Establish Interface Change Control: freeze the Spatial Query Interface specification and require formal change requests for any extensions. Audit consumer implementations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Tasking) to ensure they use only standardized queries and do not embed zone logic locally.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is clear, verifiable, and well-scoped; the DP is logically sound and complete; the IM boundary is well-defined with 0.92 containment and minimal diffusion. However, the DP-IM gate identifies five material operational/design risks (latency, scalability, reference data sync, confidence decay, interface standardization) that are not IM boundary violations but represent critical design challenges that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure safety-critical correctness and prevent diffusion during implementation. The recommended revisions are architectural in nature and require explicit functional decomposition: they cannot be addressed as implementation details alone. Proceeding to next-level decomposition is justified and necessary to manage safety criticality and containment risks.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: Specify and enforce standardized spatial query interface (containment, proximity, membership) with rigorous semantics, parameter ranges, response formats, and confidence metadata interpretation to prevent ad-hoc extensions and diffusion into consumers.

FR-2.2: Define and enforce confidence decay model and safety-critical confidence thresholds (e.g., >95% for hazard region entry) such that zone state confidence is transparent and safety-critical queries are gated by minimum confidence requirements.

FR-2.3: Implement reference data versioning, conflict detection, and resolution strategy to handle offline blueprint/CAD updates, detect conflicts with sensed reality, and resolve them without corrupting zone registry or triggering false safety alerts.

FR-2.4: Achieve latency and scalability SLAs (e.g., <100ms zone state update and spatial query response) under projected worksite complexity and telemetry frequency via hierarchical spatial indexing and edge caching.

FR-2.5: Formalize and publish zone registry data model (geometry, attributes, spatial relationships, provenance metadata, update semantics) as authoritative specification to ensure consistent zone representation and prevent local zone logic in consumers.

revise.why: No revisions to current FR, DP, or IM are required. All three gates pass. The recommended revisions from the DP-IM gate are architectural and operational in nature and are best addressed through next-level functional decomposition (sub-FRs 2.1–2.5) rather than revision of the current level. Revising the current FR, DP, or IM would be premature and would obscure the specific design challenges that must be solved.

assumptions: Safety Services IM boundary is correctly scoped and will remain singular and non-overlapping with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution orchestrator roles throughout detailed design and implementation.
Spatial query interface can be rigorously specified and frozen early without requiring ad-hoc extensions during implementation.
Reference data (blueprints, CAD models) will be available in machine-readable form and can be registered/aligned with sensed reality with acceptable accuracy and latency.
Telemetry streams from Vehicle and sensors will provide sufficient spatial coverage and frequency to support real-time zone state fusion and safety-critical queries.
Confidence decay model and safety thresholds can be defined a priori based on safety analysis and will not require runtime adaptation.
Hierarchical spatial indexing (R-tree, quadtree) and edge caching are technically feasible and will meet latency SLAs under projected worksite complexity.
open_risks: Latency bottleneck: Real-time fusion and spatial query response may not meet safety-critical timing constraints if Safety Services becomes centralized bottleneck. Mitigation strategy (hierarchical indexing, edge caching) is not yet validated against actual telemetry rates and worksite complexity.
Scalability under high-frequency telemetry: Unified registry and query engine may become computationally intensive as worksite complexity grows. Scaling strategy is not yet detailed or validated.

Reference data synchronization and staleness: Conflict resolution logic between offline reference data and sensed reality is not yet specified. Risk of corrupting zone registry or triggering false safety alerts if conflicts are not handled correctly.

Confidence metadata decay and safety-critical thresholds: Confidence decay model and minimum thresholds for safety-critical queries are not yet defined. Unclear thresholds create risk of ad-hoc consumer logic and diffusion.
Interface standardization and ad-hoc extensions: Spatial query interface specification is not yet frozen. Risk of ad-hoc extensions during implementation that diffuse zone logic into consumers.
Ownership and change control: Formal change control process for Spatial Query Interface and zone registry data model is not yet established. Risk of uncontrolled extensions and diffusion.
evidence_needed: Formal Spatial Query Interface Specification (SQI) with rigorous semantics, parameter ranges, response formats, confidence metadata interpretation, and versioning/change control policy.
Confidence Decay Model and Safety Threshold Policy document specifying decay function, minimum thresholds for safety-critical queries, and validation against safety analysis.
Reference Data Versioning and Conflict Resolution Strategy document specifying handling of offline updates, conflict detection, resolution logic, rollback, and audit trails.
Latency and Scalability SLA specification with maximum acceptable latencies for zone state updates and spatial queries, and detailed design of hierarchical spatial indexing and edge caching strategy.
Zone Registry Data Model specification (schema, spatial relationships, provenance metadata, update semantics) with formal definition and validation against FR acceptance criteria.
Interface Change Control Policy and audit results confirming that consumer implementations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Tasking) use only standardized queries and do not embed zone logic locally.
Performance testing results validating that hierarchical spatial indexing and edge caching meet latency SLAs under projected worksite complexity and telemetry frequency.
Safety analysis results (FMEA, FTA) validating that confidence thresholds and conflict resolution logic are sufficient to prevent safety-critical failures.
